name: Hiroshima Castle
access: Tram to Kamiyacho, then 15 min walk; or castle-bound bus from station
description: Rebuilt in 1958 after the atomic bomb destroyed the original, Hiroshima Castle offers exhibits on samurai life and the city's reconstruction. The surrounding moat park is one of Hiroshima's best cherry blossom destinations.

name: Hiroshima Oyster Experience
address: Various locations around Hiroshima Bay and Miyajima
access: Various — Miyajima island has multiple oyster restaurants reachable by ferry
description: Hiroshima produces 60% of Japan's oysters — the Seto Inland Sea's nutrient-rich currents grow them fat and briny. Hire a grill at a waterfront shack in Miyajima or Etajima, eat them charcoal-grilled with lemon, or raw over rice.

name: Hiroshima Peace Memorial (A-Bomb Dome)
access: Tram Line 2 or 6 from Hiroshima Station, 15 min to Genbaku Dome-mae
description: The only structure left standing near the epicenter of the world's first atomic bomb attack on August 6, 1945. Left deliberately unrestored as a permanent witness and a plea for its non-repetition. UNESCO World Heritage Site.

name: Hiroshima Peace Memorial Museum
access: Tram to Genbaku Dome-mae or Honkawa-cho; 5 min walk from dome
description: An unflinching examination of August 6, 1945 and its aftermath — personal effects of victims, charred lunchboxes, melted glass, and shadows burned into stone. One of the world's most important museums and a transformative experience.

name: Itsukushima Shrine
access: JR Sanyo Line to Miyajimaguchi, then JR ferry 10 min, ¥200
description: The great floating torii gate of Miyajima is Japan's most iconic image — a vermilion gateway standing in tidal waters, framing the island's forested mountains. At high tide it appears to float. UNESCO World Heritage and National Treasure.

name: Momijidani Park
address: 宮島町紅葉谷, Hatsukaichi City, Hiroshima
access: 15 min walk from Miyajima ferry terminal; free entry to park
description: A forest valley on Miyajima Island filled with hundreds of Japanese maple trees. Best visited in November when the crimson canopy reflects in the stream below. The ropeway to Mt. Misen starts here.

name: Onomichi
access: JR Sanyo Shinkansen to Fukuyama, then local to Onomichi (30 min)
description: A hillside port town on the Seto Inland Sea, linked by cable car to a ridge-top temple walk through 25 historic temples. The narrow lanes, black cats, and cycling culture inspired Hayao Miyazaki. Famous for its layered shoyu ramen.

name: Sandankyo Gorge
access: Bus from Hiroshima Station ~75 min (Hiroshima Bus)
description: A 16km narrow gorge in the Nishi-Chugoku Mountains carved by the Nishiki River. Pools, cascades, and overhanging walls of moss-covered granite, accessible by rowboat and cliff-side paths. Spectacular autumn foliage.

name: Shimanami Kaido
access: Start at Onomichi Station; rental bikes available from ¥1,100/day
description: A 70km cycling route connecting Honshu to Shikoku via six islands and six suspension bridges over the Seto Inland Sea — considered one of the world's top cycling routes. Views from each bridge span are extraordinary.

name: Shukkei-en Garden
access: 5 min walk from Hiroshima Station; also accessible by tram
description: A 400-year-old stroll garden in central Hiroshima — the name means 'garden of condensed landscapes' for its miniature mountains, streams, and stone islands. One of the few places in Hiroshima with direct atomic bomb survivor stories.

name: Takehara Preservation District
address: 竹原市本町, Takehara City, Hiroshima
access: JR Kure Line to Takehara Station, 1.5h from Hiroshima
description: A sake-brewing merchant town frozen in late Edo-period form — sake breweries, merchant houses with latticed facades, and a ridge-top shrine with sea views. Called the 'Little Kyoto of Hiroshima,' it sees a fraction of the crowds.

name: Tomonoura
access: Bus from Fukuyama Station (JR Shinkansen stop), 30 min
description: An ancient port town so well preserved that Miyazaki used it as reference for Ponyo. Stone lighthouse, sake breweries fermenting tidewater-cooled sake, and a hillside temple where Sakamoto Ryoma hid during the Meiji Restoration.

name: Bizen Pottery Village
access: 5 min walk from Imbe station (JR Ako Line, 45 min from Okayama)
description: The oldest continuous pottery tradition in Japan — 1,000 years of unglazed, fire-patterned ceramics. The village has 30+ active kilns to visit and workshops where you can throw your own pot.

name: Hiruzen Highlands
access: Bus from Niimi station, 45 min; car recommended
description: A cool alpine plateau at 600m with rolling meadows, Jersey dairy cows and views of the Daisen volcano. Famous for yakisoba and fresh milk soft-serve — genuinely the best in Okayama.

name: Kibiji Cycling Path
access: Rental bikes from Kibi station
description: A completely flat 17km cycling route through ancient burial mounds, rice paddies and shrine gates — the prehistoric heartland of Japan. Rental bikes, minimal cars, and café stops throughout.

name: Korakuen Garden
access: 25 min from Okayama station by bus
description: Ranked one of Japan's top three landscape gardens, this 14-hectare Edo masterpiece has rice paddies, tea houses, cranes and manicured ponds, designed to mimic a miniature countryside.

name: Kurashiki Bikan Historical Quarter
access: 15 min walk from Kurashiki station
description: White-walled Edo-period storehouses lining a willow-draped canal, now home to cafes, galleries and textile shops. The most photogenic merchant district in Japan, especially at dusk.

name: Ohama Beach, Tamano
access: 10 min from Uno station by taxi; ferry to Naoshima nearby
description: A clean white-sand beach facing the Seto Inland Sea with calm, shallow water — unusual for Okayama which has limited coastline. The sea stays warm through October.

name: Okayama Castle (Crow Castle)
access: 20 min walk from Okayama station
description: Known as the 'Crow Castle' for its striking black walls — a deliberate contrast to Himeji's white. The black exterior dominates the city skyline and pairs perfectly with Korakuen across the river.

name: Ushimado Olive Coast
access: 30 min bus from Bizen Imbe station
description: Often called 'Japan's Aegean Sea' — terraced olive groves tumbling down to a calm island-dotted bay. The Greek-influenced scenery feels entirely unlike mainland Japan.

name: Washuzan Hill
access: 40 min from Kurashiki by car
description: A modest hilltop (133m) with one of Japan's most famous panoramic views — the Seto Inland Sea scattered with 3,000 islands, crossed by the Great Seto Bridge's parallel spans.

name: Yubara Onsen (Open-Air Bath)
access: Bus from Chugoku-Katsuyama station, 30 min
description: Japan's most famous completely exposed outdoor onsen — a large riverbed bath with no walls, no roof, and the Maniwa River a metre away. You bathe under the open sky in all seasons.

name: Adachi Museum of Art
access: Free shuttle from Yasugi station, 30 min
description: Ranked Japan's best garden by the Journal of Japanese Gardening for 20 consecutive years. The 50,000m² living landscape is framed through gallery windows like painted scrolls.

name: Hinomisaki Shrine & Lighthouse
access: 25 min bus from Izumo Grand Shrine
description: A vermillion shrine complex on a wild cape — said to guard Japan from the west after sunset. A dramatic white lighthouse stands 44m above on the adjacent cliff.

name: Iwami Ginzan Silver Mine
access: Bus from Misumi station, 30 min; limited service
description: A UNESCO World Heritage mining town that once produced a third of the world's silver. Forest-covered mine tunnels, a preserved merchant town, and mountain temples create a quietly haunting atmosphere.

name: Izumo Grand Shrine
access: 25 min bus from Izumo-shi station; 90 min from Yonago
description: Japan's oldest and most sacred Shinto shrine — predating written history. All eight million Shinto deities are said to gather here every October to arrange marriages.

name: Lake Shinji at Sunset
access: 15 min walk from Matsue station to the lakeshore
description: A vast shallow lake famous for silhouettes — egrets, pine trees and fishing boats outlined against a burning sunset reflected perfectly in the still water. One of Japan's top sunset spots.

name: Matsue Castle
access: 20 min walk from Matsue station
description: One of Japan's 12 surviving original wooden castles, nicknamed 'Black Castle'. The surrounding moat is still navigable by boat — an evening tour is the most atmospheric way to see it.

name: Oni no Shitaburui Gorge
access: 30 min drive from Yasugi station; rental car recommended
description: The 'Devil's Tongue Trembling' — a river gorge where massive granite boulders crowd the riverbed so tightly that water churns through them in a continuous roar. Suspension bridges run above.

name: Tamatsukuri Onsen
access: 15 min bus from Tamatsukuri-onsen station (JR San'in Line)
admission: Foot bath free; day-use bath from ¥700
description: Japan's oldest recorded hot spring, described in 8th-century poetry as 'a single bath restores youth'. The sodium-rich waters genuinely improve skin.

name: Tsuwano — Little Kyoto of San'in
access: Bus from Tsuwano station (served from Ogori Shinkansen)
description: A mountain castle town so perfectly preserved it earned the nickname 'Little Kyoto'. A shallow channel beside the main street teems with thousands of wild koi carp — fed by locals for generations.

name: Yuushien Garden
access: Bridge from Lake Nakaumi shore; 30 min drive from Matsue
description: A lakeside garden on Daikonshima Island, famous for peonies (800 varieties, 20,000 blooms) and tree peonies in spring. In winter, the garden transforms into a magical illuminated landscape.

name: Hakuto Coast
access: 30 min drive from Tottori city; limited buses
description: The white rabbit's legendary shore — a 4km white sand beach where a divine white rabbit was saved after being deceived by sharks in Japan's oldest myth (Kojiki, 712 AD).

name: Misasa Onsen
access: 40 min bus from Kurayoshi station
admission: Public bath free; ryokan day-use from ¥700
description: One of the world's highest-radon hot spring towns, set in a mountain valley. Locals attribute the town's unusually low cancer rates to regular bathing — science backs up the claim.

name: Mount Daisen
access: Bus from Yonago station, 50 min to Daisen-ji trailhead
description: Tottori's iconic volcanic peak, called 'Hoki Fuji' for its near-perfect cone shape. Ancient beech forests cloak the lower slopes; the summit trail rewards with views over the Sea of Japan.

name: Sanbutsuji Temple Nageire-do
access: 30 min drive from Misasa Onsen; rental car recommended
description: Japan's most precarious national treasure — a small prayer hall wedged into a clifftop cave 520m up a mountain, accessible only via a challenging rope-and-chain rock climb.

name: Sand Museum Tottori
access: 20 min bus from Tottori station, adjacent to sand dunes
description: The world's only permanent sand sculpture museum, where international artists create room-sized masterpieces from local dune sand. Each year's theme changes completely.

name: Tottori Castle Ruins
access: 15 min walk from Tottori station
description: Hilltop castle ruins famous for Japan's longest siege (200+ days) before defenders surrendered to starvation in 1581. Reconstructed gates and surviving walls feel genuinely atmospheric.

name: Tottori Sand Dunes
access: 20 min bus from Tottori station
best_season: Year-round (spring for green contrast, winter for snow-dusted dunes)
description: Japan's largest sand dunes stretch 16km along the Sea of Japan coast — a genuine desert landscape of shifting ridges, camel rides and paragliding that feels deeply unlike anywhere else in Japan.

name: Uradome Coast
access: 20 min drive from Tottori city
description: One of San'in Coast's most dramatic stretches — sea caves, natural arches and hidden coves along a 15km rias coastline. Glass-bottom boat tours let you watch fish through transparent floors.

name: Yumigahama Peninsula
access: 10 min walk from Sakaiminato station
description: A 17km natural sand bar so narrow you can see both Lake Nakaumi and the Sea of Japan simultaneously. The sunset over the inland lake is especially striking.

name: Akiyoshidai Karst Plateau
access: Adjacent to Akiyoshido Cave
description: Japan's largest karst plateau, where thousands of exposed limestone pillars dot a vast green grassland. The contrast looks almost prehistoric — like standing on another planet.

name: Akiyoshido Cave
access: Bus from Yamaguchi station, 45 min
description: Japan's largest limestone cave stretches 8km, with 1km open to visitors. Stalactites, underground rivers, and cathedral chambers formed over 300 million years.

name: Chomonkyo Gorge
access: 40 min bus from Yamaguchi station
description: A 13km river gorge with 188 named rock formations, waterfalls, and emerald pools. The autumn foliage turns the canyon walls into a blaze of red and gold.

name: Hagi Castle Town
access: Bus from Hagi station, 10 min
description: One of Japan's best-preserved Edo-period castle towns, where samurai residences line winding earthen-walled lanes. Many founding fathers of modern Japan were born in these streets.

name: Karato Fish Market
access: 5 min walk from Karato Wharf; ferry from Mojiko, Fukuoka
description: Shimonoseki's legendary waterfront market where the world's most fugu (blowfish) is traded. Tuna auctions at dawn, local vendors by mid-morning — eat sashimi while watching the Kanmon Strait.

name: Kintaikyo Bridge
access: 15 min walk from Iwakuni station
description: Japan's most elegant historic bridge — five wooden arches spanning the Nishiki River without a single nail. Built in 1673, faithfully reconstructed in 1953 after a flood.

name: Motonosumi Inari Shrine
access: 30 min drive from Nagato Yumoto Onsen
description: 123 vermillion torii gates cascade down a dramatic sea cliff to a sacred sea cave. Toss a coin into the torii-top coin box for good luck — it is notoriously difficult.

name: Rurikoji Temple & Five-Story Pagoda
access: 15 min walk from Yamaguchi station
description: A five-story pagoda rising from a moss-carpeted garden pond, ranked among Japan's three most beautiful pagodas. The garden is best at dusk when stones glow warm orange.

name: Tsunoshima Bridge
access: 40 min drive from Shimonoseki
description: A 1.78km causeway connecting the mainland to Tsunoshima island, with crystal-clear turquoise water visible on both sides. Best driven at sunrise before crowds arrive.

name: Yuda Onsen
access: 5 min walk from Yuda-Onsen station
admission: Foot baths free; ryokan day-use from ¥500
description: Yamaguchi's famous hot spring town, legendary for a white fox who healed its wounds in a bubbling spring. The sodium chloride waters are excellent for skin.
